title: The Bottleneck Problem
concept: Forcing an entire sentence's meaning into a single fixed vector creates a severe bottleneck that degrades accuracy on long inputs.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is the best real-world example of a bottleneck?

options.text: A highway with ten open lanes
Pouring a whole jug of water through a narrow funnel
A sponge soaking up water in a large bucket

exercises.explain: When we force a huge amount of data through a tiny, fixed opening, we call it a bottleneck. It limits how much information can pass through.

cards.title: Squeezing Too Much In
cards.body: Have you ever tried to pack an entire week of vacation clothes into a tiny backpack? You end up leaving behind important things. Early AI models did the exact same thing with long sentences!

cards.title: What is the Bottleneck?
cards.body: In early AI translation models, the 'bottleneck problem' happens when we try to squeeze the meaning of a whole sentence—no matter how long—into a single, fixed-size list of numbers (a vector) before translating it.

cards.title: The Sticky Note Squeeze
cards.body: Imagine trying to write an entire book report on a tiny sticky note. For a short sentence, you can fit the details easily. But for a long story, you have to leave out almost everything, losing the magic of the plot.

cards.title: Short vs. Long Sentences
cards.body: If you translate 'The dog chased the cat,' a single vector easily holds that info. But if you translate a paragraph describing a dog, a cat, a mouse, a sunny day, and a red ball, the single vector runs out of space and forgets the dog entirely!

cards.title: Myth: Just Make the Vector Bigger!
cards.body: You might think making the vector larger would solve everything. But if we just make it bigger, the model becomes too slow and struggles to process short sentences. We need a smarter way, not just a bigger bucket!

cards.title: Vector Space Comparison
cards.body: See how sentence length impacts our ability to save information in a single fixed-size vector.

right.text: Overwhelms the fixed vector. Early details get pushed out and forgotten.

left.text: Easily fits into the fixed vector. All words are remembered perfectly.

exercises.prompt: What happens to translation accuracy as the input sentence gets longer in a model with a fixed vector?

options.text: Accuracy increases because the model gets more context.
Accuracy decreases because the vector cannot hold all the details.
The model runs faster but uses more memory.
Nothing changes; fixed vectors can hold infinite information.

exercises.explain: As sentences get longer, a single fixed-size vector cannot store all the details, causing the model to forget the beginning of the sentence and lose accuracy.

exercises.prompt: True or False: In basic encoder-decoder models, the vector that stores the sentence meaning automatically grows larger for longer sentences.
exercises.explain: The vector has a fixed size. It cannot grow, which is why it struggles to fit all the details of long sentences.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe this AI limitation.
exercises.sentence: Squeezing an entire long sentence into one fixed vector creates a severe ________.
exercises.explain: Forcing all information into a single vector creates a 'bottleneck' where detailed information from long inputs gets squeezed out and lost.

exercises.prompt: Which of these statements accurately describes the bottleneck problem?

options.text: AI Option A: 'Translators never forget details because the fixed vector automatically grows to match the length of any sentence.'
AI Option B: 'Squeezing a very long sentence into a single fixed vector degrades translation quality because of the bottleneck.'
AI Option C: 'The bottleneck problem only affects short sentences of three words or less.'

exercises.explain: The statement with the bottleneck is a real AI limitation. The others suggest that vectors grow infinitely or that models never forget, which is incorrect for these basic architectures.

exercises.prompt: Why does the bottleneck problem cause translation models to struggle with long sentences?

options.text: It makes the training process run too fast for the hardware to keep up.
It forces a long sentence into a single, fixed-size vector, losing early details.
It prevents the model from translating short sentences correctly.
It requires the model to use multiple vectors for a single word.

exercises.explain: The bottleneck problem occurs because standard encoder-decoder models force the entire input sequence into a single, fixed-size vector. This causes the model to forget early information when handling long sentences.
